params.text:
Personal hygiene and handwashing

People are one of the main ways bacteria get onto food. Good personal hygiene - especially thorough, frequent handwashing - is one of the simplest and most effective safeguards.

Wash your hands with warm water and soap, cover all surfaces, rinse and dry thoroughly using a clean or disposable towel. Do this:

  • before starting work and before handling food;
  • after using the toilet;
  • after handling raw meat, poultry, fish or eggs;
  • after touching bins, cleaning, coughing, sneezing or blowing your nose.

Also tie back hair, keep nails short and clean, cover cuts with a brightly coloured waterproof dressing, wear clean protective clothing, and never handle food if you have sickness or diarrhoea (you should stay off food handling until 48 hours after symptoms stop).

params.question: A care worker has just handled raw chicken and is about to prepare a sandwich for someone. What should they do first?

answers.text: Wash their hands thoroughly with warm water and soap, and use clean equipment

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Washing hands and using clean, separate equipment prevents bacteria from raw chicken contaminating the ready-to-eat sandwich.

answers.text: Wipe their hands on a tea towel and carry on

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This does not remove bacteria and can spread contamination. Hands must be washed properly with soap and water.
Gloves are not a substitute for handwashing. Hands must be washed first, and gloves changed between tasks.

answers.text: Nothing, because the sandwich will not be cooked anyway

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is exactly why care is needed. The sandwich is ready-to-eat, so any bacteria carried across on hands will not be cooked away.
